coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots as a green luminescent sensitizer to functionalize ZnO nanoparticles for enhanced photovoltaic conversion devices
coredata.dc:creator: Chava, Rama Krishna
Im, Younghwan
Kang, Misook
coredata.dc:description: A simple and facile hydrothermal approach for the synthesis of highly green luminescent nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) is reported. The prepared NCQDs show narrow size distribution of 2–3nm, abundant surface functional groups and good water solubility. To showcase the potential of these NCQDs as a solar light harvesting material, we designed NCQDs sensitized ZnO nanoparticles based photoanodes for photovoltaic solar cells. As a result, NCQDs sensitized ZnO photoanodes exhibited 75% higher photoconversion efficiency when compared to that of bare ZnO photoanodes. The characterization and optical properties of NCQDs sensitized ZnO photoanodes is also discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: A. Nanostructures
A. Semiconductors
B. Chemical synthesis
B. Luminescence
C. Photoelectron spectroscopy
C. Transmission electron microscopy(TEM)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties of NCQDs and NCQDs sensitized ZnO films
Photovoltaic measurements of NCQDs sensitized ZnO photoanodes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Typical TEM images (a,b) and physical appearance (c) of NCQDs in visible and UV light. AFM topographic image (d) and height profile (e) of as synthesized NCQDs.
TEM images of ZnO NPs (a-c), FESEM and EDS images of ZnO NPs (d, e and f), NCQDs sensitized ZnO NPs (g, h and i).
(a) X-ray diffraction patterns of ZnO and NCQDs sensitized ZnO films and (b) Raman spectrum of ZnO NPs.
High resolution and deconvoluted XPS spectra of (a) Zn-2p, (b) O-1s, (c) N–1s and (d) C–1s core levels.
UV–vis spectrum (a), photoluminescence spectrum (b) and (c) Tauc’s plot for bandgap of NCQDs; (d) absorption spectra of ZnO and NCQDs sensitized ZnO films.
J-V curves of as fabricated ZnO and NCQDs sensitized ZnO solar cells.
Photovoltaic parameters of ZnO and NCQDs sensitized ZnO NPs films.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots as a green luminescent sensitizer to functionalize ZnO nanoparticles for enhanced photovoltaic conversion devices

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Natural Science, Yeungnam University, 280 Daehak-Ro, Gyeongsan, Gyeongbuk 38541, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple and facile hydrothermal approach for the synthesis of highly green luminescent nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) is reported. The prepared NCQDs show narrow size distribution of 2–3nm, abundant surface functional groups and good water solubility. To showcase the potential of these NCQDs as a solar light harvesting material, we designed NCQDs sensitized ZnO nanoparticles based photoanodes for photovoltaic solar cells. As a result, NCQDs sensitized ZnO photoanodes exhibited 75% higher photoconversion efficiency when compared to that of bare ZnO photoanodes. The characterization and optical properties of NCQDs sensitized ZnO photoanodes is also discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: To meet the increasing demand of clean energy, the harvesting of electricity from solar energy at economically feasible cost with high efficiency is needed [1–3]. Quantum dot sensitized solar cells (QDSCs) are expected to play an important role in this revolution due to their potential in exceeding the Shockley–Queissar limit, their size-tuned optical response, and their efficient multiple carrier generation [4,5]. The important issues in the semiconductor QDSCs are their toxicity, durability and also the poor effective charge separation [6]. So an alternative to these toxic semiconductor-based QDs, currently an extensive work has been in progress to obtain non-toxic QDs for their use in QDSCs. The accidental discovery of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [7] through the purification of single walled carbon nanotubes, have become a prominent new member in the nanocarbon family [8–10]. Due to the structural and quantum confinement effects, CQDs are attracting great interest towards many applications in the fields of bio-imaging [11], drug delivery [12,13], sensors [14], and photocatalysis [15–17] due to their exceptional properties such as high intense photoluminescence (PL), high photo stability and biocompatibility. Usually CQDs are discrete and quasi-spherical shaped NPs with sizes below 10nm [18]. In comparison to traditional organic dyes and QDs, CQDs have number of benefits such as low cost, low toxicity, biocompatibility, tunable fluorescence emissions, photoresistance, and chemical inertness [19–21]. After the first report on CQDs based photocatalysts by Li’s group [22], the importance of CQDs in photocatalysis has been increased exponentially as catalysts and co-catalysts. Commonly the CQDs show a good photocatalytic performance which is directly connected to their optical/PL properties. The PL emission is produced by sp2 clusters isolated by sp3 networks in the CQDs [23,24]. Therefore, CQDs have a great potential to replace semiconductor QDs. Doping is an effective strategy to adjust the intrinsic and optical properties of CQDs, leading to improve the performance of solar energy conversion devices. Nitrogen doping has been reported to improve the optical properties and it can lower the work function of CQDs which in turn increased the photocatalytic performance [25–27].

Because of good stability and low cost materials, nanostructured oxides have been extensively studied as photoanodes in solar energy conversion devices. As a representative nanostructured metal oxide, ZnO has been widely studied because of its high electron mobility, photosensitivity and biocompatibility which can be used in many applications [28–32]. The metal oxides alone have high bandgap energies, which limit the visible light absorption resulting a low performance in the solar energy conversion processes. In order to improve the performance, mostly inorganic metal based QDs have been used. However, the surface modification of semiconductors with these metal based QDs can be toxic and therefore harmless and green sensitizers are desirable for the practical operation. Since all the optical and structural properties of CQDs/N-doped CQDs are suitable to solar energy conversion processes, the sensitization of semiconductors with these CQDs might be the right choice for fabricating the environment benign devices. Also heteronanostructures made from CQDs and semiconductors with a suitable band alignment can improve the interfacial charge-transfer processes and then promote the charge separation [33]. Recently, CQDs have been widely used in ZnO based solar energy conversion devices. For example, Mahdi et al. prepared GQDs anchored ZnO nanowire (NW) arrays and studied methylene blue photocatalytic degradation. The Photocatalytic activity of GQDs loaded ZnO NWs sample exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity when compared to pure ZnO NWs [34]. The photoelectrochemical (PEC) cell based on GQDs@ZnO NWs provided a remarkable higher photo-induced open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current density than that of plain ZnO NWs photoelectrode-based devices [17]. Other researchers also have been performed the experiments on NGQDs sensitized ZnO NWs for solar energy conversion devices. GQD and NGQD sensitized ZnO nanorods revealed a higher photoconversion efficiency than the bare ZnO nanorods and the superior photoelectrochemical behaviors are attributed to better photoabsorption and lower interfacial resistance [35–37]. The utilization of CQDs and NCQDs in solar cell devices is a rarely studied area, only very few authors established the importance of CQDs in the solar cells fabrication. For the first time, Yan et al. [38] used GQDs as a sensitizer for the solar cells. A short-circuit current density of 200μA/cm2 and an open-circuit voltage of 0.48V were produced with a fill factor of 0.58. The open-circuit voltage and the fill factor are comparable to those of cells sensitized by ruthenium complexes. These results open the exciting opportunities to tune the optical and electronic properties of GQDs for photovoltaic devices. Later, ZnO NWs-GQDs composites have been synthesized by Mrinal et al. [39] and the results confirmed that there is a charge transfer at the interface of GQDs and ZnO NWs. The ZnO NWs-GQDs composites based solar cell device shown a high open-circuit voltage of 0.8V. Therefore, alternative to dye and QD based solar cells, exploration of GQDs/CQDs as light harvesting materials becomes essential in the present scenario of energy crisis and hence we report the sensitization of ZnO NPs with nitrogen doped CQDs (NCQDs). Up to now there were no reports on the NCQDs sensitization of ZnO NPs, now we are taking this opportunity to explore the optical properties and the photovoltaic properties of NCQDs sensitized ZnO NPs. In this work, NCQDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal reaction, and were further investigated as a green sensitizer for photovoltaic devices. Photoanodes based on NCQDs sensitized ZnO NPs were prepared to fabricate photovoltaic devices, and demonstrated the importance of NCQDs sensitization in the solar energy conversion processes.

Synthesis of Nitrogen doped Carbon Quantum Dots (NCQDs): NCQDs were synthesized from citric acid and urea by a single step hydrothermal method [25]. In a typical experiment, 1mmol of citric acid and 3mmol urea were dissolved in 60ml DI water under stirring for 10min. The formed clear solution was taken into 100ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Then the autoclave was sealed and heated at 160°C for 8h. After cooling to room temperature, the final NCQDs product was collected by addition of ethanol into the solution and centrifugation at 9000rpm for 15min. The solid can be easily redispersed into water

Synthesis of ZnO NPs: ZnO NPs were synthesized by a simple ascorbic acid assisted method following a technique as reported earlier [40] but with modifications. In this method, 3.5mM ascorbic acid (AA) was added to 80ml DI water containing 0.67mM cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) under constant stirring at room temperature for 5min. Next, equal concentrations of zinc nitrate and hexamethylene tetramine (HMTA) was added while continuously stirring. The resulting solution was incubated in a closed bottle at 80°C for 8h. After the reaction was completed, the formed precipitate was washed and centrifuged several times with ethanol and DI water and dried at 60°C for overnight. Finally, the products are calcined 500°C for 2h.

A field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (HITACHI S-4800) equipped with energy dispersive X-ray (EDS) unit was used for the surface morphology, microstructural and elemental characterizations. The X-ray diffraction patterns were obtained by PANalytical X'Pert PRO X-ray diffractometer using Cu Ka (1.54056Å) radiation. Raman scattering measurements were performed using Horiba Xplora Plus Raman microscope with an excitation source of 532nm. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded using Hitachi H-7600 electron microscope. Park Systems made XE-100 model was used to get Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) images and height profile details. The BET surface area and pore size distribution of ZnO NPs was determined using Belsorp II Mini BET surface area analyzer. The chemical composition and surface analysis of NCQDs sensitized ZnO NPs film was investigated by Thermo-Scientific K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) instrument. Scinco UV–vis spectrophotometer was used to record the optical-absorption spectra of NCQDs sensitized ZnO NPs. The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of NCQDs was obtained from Horiba Sceintific spectrophotometer with He-Cd laser source at the excitation wavelength of 325nm. Photocurrent-voltage characteristics of the DSSCs were measured on Abet solar simulator under illumination with power density of AM1.5 (100mW/cm2).

The prepared ZnO NPs paste was screen printed on pre cleaned FTO glass substrates using adhesive scotch tape as a frame and spacer. The films were then dried and calcined at 400°C for 1h to remove any organic materials from the pastes. After cooling to room temperature, the ZnO photoelectrodes were immersed into NCQDs solution for different times. The electrodes loaded with NCQDs were then washed and dried in the air before cell assembly. The counter electrode of DSSCs was made of FTO glass onto which Pt catalysts were sputtered for 1min. The ZnO photoelectrode and the Pt counter electrode were sandwiched together with a 60 mm-thick hot-melting surlyn spacer in between, and the space between the electrodes was filled with an electrolyte containing 0.1M lithium iodide (LiI, Sigma-Aldrich), 0.6M 1,2-dimethyl-3-propylimidazolium iodide (PMII, Merk), 0.05M I2, and 0.5M tert-butylpyridine in acetonitrile.

For the preparation of NCQDs, citric acid and urea were put into DI water and hydrothermal reaction was carried out for 8h. After cooling, the solution was centrifuged and mixed with DI water. The resultant NCQDs solution is in brown colour and details are given in Experimental Section. The TEM images of NCQDs with different magnifications are given in Fig. 1 a,b. The prepared NCQDs are in quite uniform size of around ∼2nm. Fig. 1c shows the physical appearance of NCQDs solution in visible light and UV light. High intense green luminescent emission was observed with the UV light which can be beneficial for extending the solar energy spectrum of semiconductor devices. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image provided in Fig. 1d shows that the synthesized NCQDs having quasi-spherical shape and Fig. 1e represents the corresponding height profile of selected NCQDs indicating that the topographic height of the NCQDs is mostly less than 4nm.

Next, HMTA based simple precipitation reaction was used to synthesize ZnO NPs. The morphologies of the samples were observed using SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 (a-c), the ZnO NPs are in semi-spherical shape having a diameter of 25–30nm. Fig. 2(d,e) illustrate the surface morphology of ZnO NPs films. After NCQDs sensitization of ZnO NPs films, the FE-SEM images are shown in Fig. 2(g,h). In order to check the elemental composition of the films, we have taken EDS spectra before and after NCQDs sensitization and the respective images are given in Fig. 2(f and i). These EDS spectra confirmed that the ZnO films are composed of Zn and O elements only and whereas the NCQDs sensitized films showed N, C, Zn and O elements. From Fig. 2f, it was confirmed that NCQDs are covered on the surface of ZnO NPs. The weight and atomic percentages (%) of ZnO and NCQDs sensitized ZnO NPs films are also provided in Fig. 2(f and i).

Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) was used to characterize the crystallinity and phase purity of as made ZnO films. Fig. 3 a shows the XRD spectra of ZnO and NCQDs sensitized ZnO films with different sensitized times. Several well defined diffraction reflections characteristic of ZnO and FTO substrate are clearly observed in all films. The diffractions patterns observed at the angles of 31.9, 34.5, 36.4, 47.7, 56.7, 63.1, 68.0, and 69.2° can be assigned to the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (112), and (201) planes of the hexagonal wurtzite ZnO (JCPDS: 00-003-0888), respectively. In addition to these, the patterns observed at 26.7, 33.8, 37.8, 51.6, 54.9, 61.9, and 65.6° can be assigned to the (110), (101), (200), (211), (220), (310), and (112) planes of SnO2 (JCPDS: 00-002-1340), respectively. After the sensitization with NCQDs, an extra broad peak at about 16.5° also observed in all NCQDs sensitized ZnO films by retaining ZnO diffraction planes. To verify this, the X-ray diffraction pattern of alone NCQDs also recorded and given in Fig. S1. This broad peak is attributed to the NCQDs. As seen from Fig. 3a, as the NCQDs sensitization time increases, the intensity of diffraction patterns of ZnO planes are decreasing due to the fact that more number of NGQDs are covered on the surface of ZnO NPs.

In order to confirm the purity and structural features of as synthesized ZnO NPs, we have also measured the Raman spectrum and shown in Fig. 3b. Since the ZnO belongs to C4 6v symmetry with space group P63 mc, the number of atoms per unit cell is four and hence the number of phonon modes is 12. As per the group theory, the normal lattice vibration modes from ZnO can be written as follows [28],
(1) Γopt =A1 +2B1 +E1 +E2

Non-polar optical phonon modes with symmetry E2 can be divided into two frequencies: high (E2H) and low (E2L) frequency phonons. The E2H mode is associated with the oxygen atoms; and whereas the E2L mode is associated with the vibration of the heavy Zn sublattice. The B1 modes are silent, and the others are Raman active. The other Raman modes A1 and E1 are polar, infrared active and split into the transverse optical (TO) and longitudinal optical (LO) phonon modes. All the observed vibrational modes in Fig. 3b are assigned to ZnO wurtzite structure. The Raman spectrum of ZnO NPs exhibited various vibrational modes at 98, 330, 381, 437 and 576cm−1 which were assigned to E2(low), (E2H-E2L), A1(TO), E2(high), E1(LO) vibrational modes. The two strong vibration modes observed at 98 and at 437cm−1 are assigned to the two nonpolar optical phonon (E2) modes of ZnO at low and high frequencies, respectively [41]. The Raman mode at 437cm−1 is the characteristic of oxygen vibrations [42] and which further confirms the prepared ZnO NPs are of high quality. The peak at 330cm−1 was attributed to the multi-phonon scattering process (E2H-E2L) [43]. The E1(LO) phonon band observed at 576cm−1 has been attributed to the formation of oxygen defects and interstitial Zn sites [44,45].

More information about the oxidation states and the chemical compositions of NCQDs modified ZnO film was obtained from XPS analysis. Fig. S2 presents the surface survey scan spectrum of NCQDs sensitized ZnO film. The survey spectrum noticed the elements Zn, O, N, and C with the atomic percentages of 37.85, 40.04, 2.56, and 19.56% respectively. The high resolution XPS spectra of Zn, O, N, and C elements are given in Fig. 4 . The Zn-2p core level XPS spectrum of NCQDs sensitized ZnO film is given in Fig. 4a. The peaks at 1022.21eV and 1045.21eV correspond to Zn-2p3/2 and Zn-2p1/2, respectively. The energy difference between the two Zn 2p peaks is 23eV and indicates that the oxidation state of Zn is +2 [34,46]. The deconvoluted O–1s spectrum of ZnO is shown in Fig. 4b. The peak observed at 530.9eV is assigned to the lattice oxygen in ZnO chemical boding. The other peak attained at 532.3eV is attributed to CO bonding. As the ZnO film is sensitized with NCQDs, we are also attempted to determine the features of N and C elements and detailed peak fitting analysis was performed. The corresponding spectra of N and C elements are given in Fig. 4c and d. As shown in Fig. 4c, the high resolution XPS spectrum of N–1s was fitted to four surface components centered at 398.5, 399.5, 400.3 and 401.2eV corresponding to CN, NH2, CNC, and NC3 respectively [47,48]. Fig. 4d elucidates the high resolution XPS spectrum of C–1s element and deconvoluted into five surface components at 284.6, 285.4, 286.5, 288.5 and 289.5eV corresponding to sp2 (CC), sp3 (CC, CH), CO/CN, CO/CN, and OCO [25,47–49]. These surface components of NCQDs as observed by XPS were in good agreement with the other reported results.

In order to calculate the surface area and pore size distribution of ZnO NPs, N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms are recorded and are given in Fig. S3. As seen in Fig. S2, the ZnO NPs showed a strong adsorption and desorption phenomena which are characteristics of mesoporous materials and are attributed to Type-III isotherms. From N2 adsorption-desorption data, BET surface area calculated as 13.85m2/g. The BJH pore size distribution analysis shows that the ZnO NPs having a mesopore size of 9.5nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of NCQDs and NCQDs sensitized ZnO films
ce:section.ce:para: The optical absorption behavior of NCQDs was displayed in Fig. 5 . Usually, the CQDs show a strong optical absorption in the UV region and with a tail spreading out into the visible range. As shown in Fig. 5a, the absorption spectrum of NCQDs exhibited a strong a peak at 338nm which is the characteristic feature of hydrothermally grown NCQDs. This absorption peak at 338nm is assigned to n→π* electron transition in CO groups [25,50,51]. Another most important fascinating feature of NCQDs is their photoluminescence (PL). Different sized CQDs shows different PL properties depending on their synthesis procedures. The luminescence property has been proposed to arise from emissive traps, excitons of carbon, quantum confinement effect, oxygen-containing groups, aromatic structures, free zigzag sites and edge defects [22,52,53]. Fig. 5b reveals the emission spectrum of NCQDs under the excitation wavelength of 325nm. The NCQDs exhibited two emission bands at 450 and 570nm. The several kinds of functional groups existing on the surface of NCQDs have different surface state energy levels, which result in a series of emissive traps [48]. However the origin of green emission (Fig. 1c) and the different wavelength excitation based emission studies are in progress. Next, the optical absorption properties of NCQDs sensitized ZnO photoanodes were observed by UV–vis light absorption measurements. The energy bandgap (Eg) value is calculated by using Tauc’s relation [54,55], αhν =A(hν −Eg)n, where α is the absorption coefficient, hν is the photon energy, Eg is the optical band gap, and n equals 1/2 or 2 for direct and indirect allowed transition, respectively. From the UV–VIS absorption data and Tauc’s equation, the bandgap curve was plotted and is shown in Fig. 5c. The direct optical bandgap for the synthesized NCQDs was determined as ∼2.73eV. Fig. 5d describes the absorption and reflection spectra (Fig. S4) of NCQDs sensitized ZnO photoanodes respectively. It is observed that the bare ZnO photoanode has a strong light absorption at wavelengths below 380nm. After NCQDs sensitization, a small finite absorbance/reflectance at wavelengths greater than 400nm for the ZnO NPs could be ascribed to light scattering. After NCQDs immobilization on the ZnO NPs, significantly enhanced visible-light absorption at wavelengths beyond 400nm is observed, indicating large NCQDs enhanced visible-light absorption

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photovoltaic measurements of NCQDs sensitized ZnO photoanodes
ce:section.ce:para: The NCQDs sensitized ZnO NP films are used as photoanodes in solar cells fabricated as given in the experimental section. ZnO NPs paste was screen printed on FTO substrate and then sensitized with NCQDs at different times. The current density versus voltage (J-V) curves for the fabricated cells are shown in Fig. 6 . The photovoltaic parameters for various photoanodes are summarized in Table 1 . From Fig. 6, it is clearly observed that there is an effect of NCQDs on the photovoltaic performance of ZnO photoanodes. The current density (Jsc) increases with the NCQDs sensitization time since the number of NCQDs anchoring on ZnO NPs is increases. The current density value reaches a maximum value of 5.48mA·cm−2 at 30min NCQDs sensitization, and then decreases as the sensitization time further increases. This phenomenon is might be due to the competition between photon adsorption and charge recombination. The efficient anchoring of QDs on the surface of ZnO NPs would be required to provide a better performance than that of bare ZnO NPs. As seen from Table 1, it is found that bare ZnO photoanode shows a photoconversion efficiency (PCE) of 0.88% whereas the optimized ZnO-NCQDs@30min cell shows a PCE of 1.18% efficiency, which is 74% increment to the ZnO photoanodes. The enhancement in current density and then PCE is an evidence of NCQDs sensitization which can increases the light harvesting capacity of ZnO photoanodes. These results clearly validate that the performance of ZnO photoanodes can be significantly improved by NCQDs. Generally, the PCE of ZnO-NCQDs solar cell seriously relies on the photo-absorption, charge separation, and following charge-transfer process at the photoanode. Based on the optical absorption data (Fig. 5) and the energy levels of both ZnO and NCQDs, the following analysis could be proposed. The lower PCE of the ZnO NPs-based devices is likely due to the poor visible light absorption from its large bandgap (∼3.3eV). In the case of NCQDs@ZnO NPs-based devices under light illumination, electron–hole pairs are produced by the NCQDs. The difference in their bandgaps, could allow the transfer of photo-generated electrons from NCQDs to ZnO. The good anchoring of NCQDs on ZnO NPs together with the suitable work function of could make it effective for charge separation and transfer of the electrons to the electrode surface, thus resulting in higher performance of ZnO-NCQDs solar cells [17,56]. The performance of these NCQDs sensitized solar cells can be improved by optimizing the device fabrications and the light absorption properties of NCQDs.

In summary, we have established the use of NCQDs as a green sensitizer to fabricate the ZnO-NCQDs photoanodes in the solar cells. The ZnO NPs photoanodes are sensitized with NCQDs at different immersion times. The photoanodes based on ZnO-NCQDs exhibited a significantly higher photo-current density/photo-conversion efficiency than that of bare ZnO NPs photoanode devices. The anchored NCQDs significantly enhanced the visible light absorption and induced the effective charge separation. The heterostructures fabricated from NCQDs and other semiconductors possess unique interfacial regions that assist the charge separation and increase the concentration of charge carriers available in the photoreactions. This method could also useful to fabricate NCQDs based semiconductor composites for other efficient solar energy conversion devices and sensing applications.

X-ray diffraction pattern of NCQDs, XPS surface survey scan spectrum of ZnO-NCQDs film, N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of ZnO NPs and reflectance spectra of ZnO and NCQDs sensitized ZnO photoanodes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Basic Research Science and Technology Projects through the National Research Foundation of Korea Grant funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT & Future Planning (No: 2015R1A1A3A04001268), for which authors are very grateful.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.materresbull.2017.06.040 .

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
